era_context: The late 15th century saw the consolidation of early modern European nation-states, Ottoman expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean, Ming Dynasty stability in China, and the final stages of the Reconquista in Iberia. This was a period of dynastic succession, religious conflict, and the early Age of Exploration.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (beyond Portuguese coastal trading posts)

events.title: Death of Charles the Bold of Burgundy; Succession Crisis
events.description: Charles the Bold, Duke of Burgundy, died in the Battle of Nancy on January 5, 1477, fighting against the Swiss and French. His death marked the end of Burgundian independence as a major power. His daughter Mary of Burgundy inherited the duchy, but faced immediate threats from King Louis XI of France, who seized much Burgundian territory. Mary's marriage to Maximilian of Habsburg (Holy Roman Emperor) in 1477 was a crucial response to this crisis, linking Burgundy to the Habsburg dynasty and preventing French annexation. This succession crisis reshaped the political map of Western Europe.
events.certainty_note: Charles the Bold's death at Nancy on January 5, 1477, is well-documented. Mary's marriage to Maximilian occurred in August 1477.

events.title: Marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian I
events.region: Duchy of Burgundy / Holy Roman Empire
events.description: Mary of Burgundy married Maximilian I, Archduke of Austria and future Holy Roman Emperor, in August 1477. This marriage was a strategic response to the French threat following Charles the Bold's death. The union linked Burgundy to the Habsburg dynasty and prevented French annexation of Burgundian territories. This marriage had profound consequences for European politics, as it established the Habsburg claim to the Low Countries and set the stage for Habsburg dominance in the 16th century.
events.certainty_note: The marriage date of August 1477 is well-documented in multiple sources.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War Continues
events.description: The Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) continued with Ottoman forces maintaining pressure on Venetian holdings in the Aegean and Adriatic. In 1477, Ottoman naval dominance was evident, and Venice was increasingly forced to negotiate. The war was economically draining for Venice and militarily advantageous for the Ottomans.
events.certainty_note: The war's continuation in 1477 is well-documented in both Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Consolidation Under Chenghua Emperor
events.description: The Chenghua Emperor (r. 1464–1487) continued to consolidate Ming Dynasty power in 1477. The dynasty was stable, and the emperor was focused on internal administration and maintaining the tributary system. This period saw continued economic development and cultural flourishing in China, though the dynasty was beginning to show signs of the bureaucratic rigidity that would characterize later Ming rule.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: General Ming Dynasty stability is well-documented; specific events for 1477 are not prominent in surviving records.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration Continues
events.description: Portuguese explorers continued systematic exploration of the West African coast under King John II. Portuguese traders were establishing trading posts and forts, acquiring gold, ivory, and enslaved people. This exploration was laying the groundwork for the later Portuguese maritime empire and the transatlantic slave trade.

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and merchant accounts

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast is well-documented; specific events for 1477 are less detailed.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Charles the Bold's death is sometimes overstated as the 'end of Burgundy'; in fact, Burgundy remained a significant power under Mary and Maximilian, and the Burgundian territories became even more important as part of the Habsburg inheritance. The marriage of Mary and Maximilian is sometimes portrayed as a purely strategic move, but modern scholarship emphasizes the personal and dynastic dimensions of the union. The Venetian-Ottoman War is sometimes presented as a clear Ottoman victory, but Venice's negotiating position was stronger than it might appear, and the treaty of 1479 preserved significant Venetian interests.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1477 is rich in Western European sources, particularly Burgundian, French, and Italian. Ottoman sources are available for military campaigns. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed but focus on court politics. African, American, and Asian sources beyond China are sparse or absent. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events in Europe; economic and social change in Africa and Asia are underrepresented. The death of Charles the Bold is exceptionally well-documented due to its dramatic nature and impact on European politics; this may create a distorted sense of its significance relative to other events.

graph_edges.note: Charles the Bold's death led to Mary's marriage to Maximilian
Mary and Maximilian's marriage established Habsburg claim to Low Countries
Continued Ottoman pressure led to Treaty of Constantinople
